item.title: My attempt at restoring a $20 MacBook (Part 1)
item.description:
A few weeks ago, I came across a mid 2012 unibody Macbook Pro, the same model that I grew up using, for only $20. Granted, on the listing it said that it didn't work, as there was no hard drive or RAM inside. Luckily, I still have access to all of the parts from my old MBP, even though it also doesn't work anymore. I figured that I could replace the non-working or missing parts from the eBay MBP with the still-working parts on my old MBP to get the machine working.

It arrived today. The first thing I did when I got it out of the packaging was open it up and put in a SATA SSD that I had laying around, as well as the 4GB of RAM that my old MBP originally came with. A lot of the screws on the bottom cover were missing, with only 4 left on the corners. So for now, I decided to put tape over the empty screw holes, so that dust wouldn't get into the internals--one of the issues I ran into with my old MBP was that the rubber feet came off, letting dust and other gunk in that would later damage the SATA cable.

After closing it back up, I tried to charge the laptop. The only charger I had for it was a very janky and old one with a lot of exposed wire, so I covered the exposed parts with tape and tried to plug the laptop in. It already had an aftermarket battery, which I was a little glad about because the only other battery I had (the original one that came with my MBP) was probably in much worse shape. However, the listing said that the laptop wouldn't charge at all - the light wouldn't even come on, so I figured there was a problem with the charging port. I followed this guide to replacing the charging port, using the one from my old machine, to no avail. It still wouldn't charge.

This led me to believe that maybe there was an issue with my old charger. It's a wonder it didn't explode or catch fire lmao. I guess I should've made sure I had a working charger for this device before I ordered it or while I was waiting for it to arrive. But I found an old MagSafe 1 charger in much better condition that'll arrive in a few days, so I'll update when it arrives.

item.title: My Framework 13 setup as a college student
item.description:
For most of my last year of high school, I have been using a ThinkPad X380 Yoga that I bought used from eBay a few years ago, and it’s an alright machine for schoolwork, but it had a graphics issue where all the text renders really weirdly, and nothing I’ve done (switching OSes, desktops etcetc) has been able to fix the issue, except for turning off the graphics drivers, but that makes the computer run slower & completely shut down every time I close the lid. So a few weeks ago, as a graduation gift, I received the Framework Laptop 13, and I absolutely adore it.

Putting it together and setting it up was really straightforward, and after using it for a few weeks, it reminds me a lot of the old mid 2012 MacBook Pro that I used before my old ThinkPad. The build quality is really nice, and this might be kind of a random detail but I really love the feel of the trackpad. I love that it actually clicks (haptic trackpads aren’t really for me haha) but it doesn’t have too much give. I’ve used laptops with trackpads that are SUPER like firm on the top but have way too much give in the bottom area, and it makes the whole laptop feel really cheap, but this doesn’t have that. The top area is slightly firmer than the bottom area, but not by a whole lot.

Obviously, I’m also a huge fan of how repairable and upgradable it is too. I originally ordered this laptop to have 8GB of RAM, because of RAM prices of course and since that’s what I had on my ThinkPad, but that quickly showed to not be enough for me, even on Linux. So I ordered another 8GB stick of RAM from eBay using some of my graduation money, opened the laptop up, and installed the new (used) stick in the other empty channel for a total of 16GB. And that’s all that it took. They even made it so that you don’t have to disconnect the battery.

I originally installed Fedora Linux, because it’s officially supported by Framework, and I really liked it. It’s a super smooth experience to install and use. But I’m taking a math class over the summer for college, and it requires Lockdown Browser for the exam. Lockdown Browser doesn’t support Linux, even with WINE, and it detects if it’s being run in a VM (ugh, I know) so I originally was just going to install Windows 10 on my ThinkPad and use it to take the exam. But then I realized that in all of the time that I’ll spend in college, I’ll probably end up taking more classes that require Lockdown Browser or similar software for tests, and I don’t want to carry around 2 laptops with me all the time, because then that’s 2 laptops that could get stolen instead of just one. So I decided to just bite the bullet and install Windows 11 on my Framework, debloat it, and set up dualboot later if Windows is still too enshittified for me.

And then I got carried away installing games that I hadn’t played in years because none of the stuff I owned was fast enough, or they didn’t work under WINE, or some other reason, and I discovered two things: One, my favorite games took up a lot of storage, so much that dualboot isn’t a great option, and two, Windows 11 is actually really nice to use once it’s fully debloated and customized. But I still really like Linux. So I decided that I’m still going to set up dualboot at some point in the future, but this is how I’ll probably do it: When Framework restocks the storage expansion cards, I’ll order one and use that to store all of my files, and maybe a few games. And then I’ll partition the internal NVME and use that to run both OSes, but only the OSes and software. That way I have easy access to all of my stuff across both OSes, but I don’t have to worry about storage because I’ll have that in abundance. Really it would be nice if I didn’t have to do all this, because remote proctoring sucks, and Microsoft still sucks, but it’s nice to have this option.
